title: The CIRP Process
content:
Session 4: CIRP Process 
Gujarat Urja Vikas Nigam Limited v Amit Gupta [2021 | SC | 2J] 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã GUVNL was the OC ‚Äì CD generated solar power; its ONLY commercial contract was a PPA with GUVNL. 
‚û¢‚Äã Under the PPA, GUVNL purchased all electricity generated by the CD. 
‚û¢‚Äã Article 9.2.1(e) of the PPA was an ipso facto clause, allowing automatic termination if the CD entered insolvency 
proceedings and it wasn't resolved within 30 days. 
‚ùñ‚Äã CD notified GUVNL about plant disruption due to floods. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Due to financial stress caused by the disruptions and damage, for which insurance claims remained pending, CD was unable 
to fully service its debt to the financing party ‚Üí was declared NPA. 
‚ùñ‚Äã CD voluntarily initiated CIRP by filing Section 10, IBC; NCLT admitted it, declared moratorium, and appointed IRP 
[Amit Gupta ‚Äì Respondent here]. 
‚ùñ‚Äã GUVNL issued Default Notices invoking Article 9.2.1(e) (termination triggered solely due to insolvency). 
‚ùñ‚Äã IRP received notices ‚Üí IRP + RP filed Section 60(5) IBC applications before NCLT, seeking injunction against 
termination. 
‚ùñ‚Äã NCLT passed an interim stay on termination ‚Üí held that PPA is an instrument which can‚Äôt have effect over IBC since it‚Äôs 
inconsistent with the same. 
‚û¢‚Äã Under Final Order ‚Üí termination set aside; PPA protected. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
However, NCLT added that PPA could be terminated if liquidation begins ‚Üí appealed to SC. 
‚ùñ‚Äã NOTE: PPA was the CD‚Äôs only contract and sole revenue source ‚Üí termination would cause corporate death, not just 
reduced value. 
 
Issues: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Scope of NCLT‚Äôs jurisdiction under Section 60(5)(c) ‚Üí Can it intervene in contract termination disputes like PPA? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Validity and enforceability of ipso facto clauses in India ‚Üí Can termination solely triggered by insolvency be upheld? 
 
Rules: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 60(5)(c), IBC ‚Üí NCLT can decide any question of law/fact arising out of or relating to insolvency proceedings. 
‚û¢‚Äã
 
37 
 
‚û¢‚Äã
 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 14(1) + Explanation ‚Üí Licenses, permits, quotas, concessions, or similar rights cannot be terminated due to 
insolvency, if current dues are paid. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 14(2) ‚Üí Essential supplies cannot be terminated during moratorium. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 14(2A) ‚Üí Goods/services critical to preserving the CD as a going concern cannot be terminated, unless 
post-moratorium dues unpaid. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 238 ‚Üí The provisions of this Code shall have effect, notwithstanding anything inconsistent therewith contained in 
any other law for the time being in force or any instrument having effect by virtue of any such law. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Arcelor Mittal precedent ‚Üí Section 60(5) gives exclusive jurisdiction to NCLT for insolvency-linked disputes. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Moratorium is in rem (Mokal). 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã NCLT has wide but not infinite jurisdiction 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 60(5)(c) grants residuary jurisdiction where the dispute: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
arises solely from insolvency, and 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
has a direct nexus to CIRP survival or debtor rehabilitation. 
‚û¢‚Äã Prevents parallel proceedings, forum fragmentation, and loss of asset value due to delay ‚Üí but NCLT cannot 
encroach into matters unrelated to insolvency. 
‚û¢‚Äã HERE ‚Äì jurisdiction justified 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The PPA was terminated only because CIRP began, not for an independent commercial breach. [can use 
this to distinguish exam problems] 
‚óè‚Äã
Without insolvency, no termination right would have arisen. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Therefore, the dispute arises solely from insolvency ‚Üí satisfies 60(5)(c). 
‚ùñ‚Äã Instrument ‚Üí doesn‚Äôt necessarily need to be a creation of a statute, so long as it comes into effect/becomes enforceable by a 
statutory law ‚áí the bilateral contract here was  
‚ùñ‚Äã Not laying down a general rule ‚Üí case turns on exceptional facts 
‚û¢‚Äã Court issues a major caution: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Intervention is justified only because the PPA was the CD‚Äôs only contract. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Termination here meant corporate death, not mere diminution in value. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
NCLT cannot set aside all ipso facto terminations in ordinary cases. 
‚û¢‚Äã Future test ‚Üí NCLT can interfere only if the contract‚Äôs termination would make corporate death inevitable, 
not merely weaken business. 
‚ùñ‚Äã IBC‚Äôs objective: preserve going concern‚Äôs value 
‚û¢‚Äã CIRP is meant for reorganisation, not dismantling. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Ending the only revenue contract would ‚Äúpull the rug out from under CIRP‚Äù and make resolution 
impossible. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Liquidation is only the last resort [Swiss Ribbons] 
‚û¢‚Äã Goal of moratorium + CIRP = status quo, revival environment, breathing space (‚Äúcalm period‚Äù). 
‚ùñ‚Äã Legislative scheme does NOT expressly invalidate ipso facto clauses 
‚û¢‚Äã IBC does not contain a blanket ban on ipso facto clauses. 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 14 gives limited protection, e.g.: 
38 
 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Government licenses, permits, quotas (Explanation to 14(1)) 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Essential and critical supplies (14(2), 14(2A)) 
‚û¢‚Äã But contracts like PPA are not per se protected in statute. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Therefore, validity of ipso facto clauses remains unclear in Indian law ‚Üí Court left the issue open for 
Parliament to legislate. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Court refuses to bring back SICA through the backdoor 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 22 of SICA once allowed broad suspension of contracts ‚Üí the Court explicitly cautioned against 
recreating that regime judicially. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Intervention here was not to revive SICA logic, but to prevent certain corporate death in extraordinary 
facts. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Resolution bias (rather than liquidation bias) dominates 
‚û¢‚Äã The Court preferred value preservation over strict contractual enforcement. 
‚û¢‚Äã PPA is the ‚Äúsheet anchor‚Äù of the business ‚Üí its survival = CIRP survival. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 60(5)(c) jurisdiction is wide but insolvency-linked, not universal. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Contract terminations solely because of CIRP can be restrained only if: 
‚û¢‚Äã the termination flows directly from insolvency, and 
‚û¢‚Äã it would cause corporate death, not just loss of value. 
‚ùñ‚Äã IBC has limited statutory protection against ipso facto clauses (s.14 only partially covers it). 
‚ùñ‚Äã No blanket invalidation of ipso facto clauses in India yet ‚Üí Parliament must clarify. 
 
Comments:  
‚ùñ‚Äã Moral Hazard ‚Üí Promoters may rely on court protection instead of resolving debts. 
‚û¢‚Äã Softens the strict creditor-in-control model of IBC by judicially shielding commercial contracts. 
‚û¢‚Äã Echoes Section 22 of SICA, despite the Court warning against its return. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Risks shifting IBC from a market-led insolvency process to a discretion-driven, debtor-protective regime. 
 
Arcelormittal India Private Limited v Satish Kumar Gupta [2018 | SC | 2J] 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Corporate Debtor (CD): Essar Steel India Ltd. (ESIL) 
‚ùñ‚Äã Financial Creditors: SBI, Standard Chartered ‚Üí Section 7 CIRP admitted by NCLT. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Two resolution applicants emerged: 
‚û¢‚Äã ArcelorMittal India Pvt Ltd (AMIPL) 
‚û¢‚Äã Numetal Ltd. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 29A was inserted on 18 Jan 2018 (IBC Amendment Act 8 of 2018), disqualifying persons connected to NPAs for 1+ 
year from submitting resolution plans. 
‚û¢‚Äã Disqualification findings by the Resolution Professional: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
AMIPL 
‚óè‚Äã
Its connected person = ArcelorMittal Netherlands (holding co.) 
‚óè‚Äã
AM Netherlands was promoter of Uttam Galva Steels, whose account was an NPA for >1 year. 
‚óè‚Äã
Though AM Netherlands sold shares just before the resolution plan, stock exchange 
de-classification was not approved yet ‚Üí it was still a promoter in law ‚Üí barred under 29A(c). 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Numetal 
‚óè‚Äã
Incorporated only 7 days before submitting EOI. 
‚óè‚Äã
Shareholder Aurora Enterprises [AEL] (which holds 25% of Numetal) was wholly owned by 
Rewant Ruia, son of Ravi Ruia (promoter of ESIL, an NPA account holder). 
‚óè‚Äã
Found to be ‚Äúacting in concert‚Äù with a disqualified promoter ‚Üí barred under 29A(c). 
‚ùñ‚Äã NCLT upheld disqualification ‚Üí NCLAT dismissed appeal ‚Üí matter reached Supreme Court. 
39 
 
 
 
 
Issues: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether AMIPL and Numetal were ineligible under Section 29A(c) (persons connected to NPAs, acting jointly/in concert, 
promoter/management/control)? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the corporate veil can be pierced to identify the real controlling persons behind a resolution applicant? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the stage of ineligibility of the resolution applicant under S.29A(c) of the Code attaches at the date of 
commencement of the CIRP or at the time when the resolution plan is submitted? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether SC could grant relief using Article 142 despite disqualification? 
 
Rule: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 29A(c), IBC ‚Üí Bars persons (or those acting jointly/in concert with them) if they are: 
‚û¢‚Äã promoter / in control / management of a company, and 
‚û¢‚Äã its account has been an NPA for ‚â• 1 year before CIRP commenced. 
‚û¢‚Äã Proviso: eligibility can be restored by clearing all overdue NPA amounts before submitting the resolution plan. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 5(25), IBC ‚Üí Defines ‚ÄúResolution Applicant‚Äù. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 2(27), Companies Act, 2013 ‚Üí Defines ‚Äúcontrol‚Äù (includes ability to influence management or policy decisions ‚Äì de 
facto or de jure). 
‚ùñ‚Äã SEBI Takeover Code (Reg 2(1)(q)) ‚Üí Defines ‚Äúpersons acting in concert‚Äù in broad, non-exhaustive terms, including 
relatives and informal cooperation toward common control. 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Purpose of Section 29A = Unclean hands must stay out 
‚û¢‚Äã The provision is rooted in the principle that defaulting or responsible promoters should not regain control of 
the asset through CIRP ‚Üí purposive interpretation, not literal interpretation, to prevent back-door entry. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Corporate veil must be pierced in insolvency 
‚û¢‚Äã Normally, shareholders ‚âÝ company (Salomon principle). 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
But when a company is formed to submit a resolution plan, the Court is duty-bound to see who actually 
controls it. 
‚óè‚Äã
Since 29A targets de facto control, the Court will look past formal structures to identify 
the real decision-makers. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Hence, veil piercing was not only permitted but necessary. 
40 
 
‚ùñ‚Äã ‚ÄúActing jointly‚Äù and ‚Äúin concert‚Äù are interpreted broadly 
‚û¢‚Äã No need for a formal joint venture ‚Üí acting together in any manner is enough. 
‚û¢‚Äã SEBI definition applies ‚Üí includes: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
relatives (parent/child), 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
de facto cooperation to influence a company, and 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
indirect arrangements. 
‚û¢‚Äã Applying this: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
AM Netherlands and AMIPL were acting together ‚Üí disqualification travels downstream. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Rewant Ruia (Numetal) acting with father Ravi Ruia (promoter of defaulting CD) ‚Üí disqualified. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Promoter status is factual, not cosmetic 
‚û¢‚Äã AM Netherlands sold shares days before submission, but still listed as promoter pending regulatory approval ‚Üí it 
was a sham transaction to escape 29A, and promoter status had not ended in law. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Control means positive, not negative control 
‚û¢‚Äã ‚ÄúControl‚Äù = proactive ability to influence management or policy ‚Üí merely being able to block decisions 
(negative control) is not control. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The Court applied noscitur a sociis ‚Üí ‚Äúmanagement‚Äù and ‚Äúcontrol‚Äù must both mean positive influence, 
not passive power. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Timing of disqualification 
‚û¢‚Äã The opening words of S.29A state that ‚Äúa person shall not be eligible to submit a resolution plan‚Äù 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Clear that the stage of ineligibility attaches when the plan is submitted and not when the CIRP is 
commenced. 
‚û¢‚Äã The 1-year NPA period is counted backwards from CIRP commencement. 
‚ùñ‚Äã RP is required to examine that the plan submitted by various applicants is complete in all respects, before submitting it to 
the CoC ‚Üí the RP is not required to take any decision 
‚û¢‚Äã Even though it is not necessary for the RP to give reasons while submitting the plan to the CoC, it would be in the 
fitness of things if he appends to the recommendation the DD done by him to the CoC. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Article 142 Relief (controversial) 
‚û¢‚Äã Even after holding AMIPL ineligible, the Court used Article 142 to grant: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
2 weeks to AM Netherlands to clear all NPA dues 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
If paid, disqualification would be cured. 
‚û¢‚Äã This was criticised for: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
reviving an ineligible bidder despite legislative intent, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
undermining competitive neutrality, and 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
going beyond the statute rather than supplementing it (contrary to SCBA v Union of India test). 
‚ùñ‚Äã Time spent in bona fide litigation beyond 270 days may be excluded to prevent justice from being defeated by procedure. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Both AMIPL and Numetal were correctly disqualified under Section 29A(c). 
‚ùñ‚Äã Corporate veil can and must be pierced to identify persons acting jointly or in concert. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Promoter/control analysis is based on real influence, not form. 
‚ùñ‚Äã SC allowed a one-time chance under Article 142 for AM to cure its NPA defaults by paying dues, a move criticised as judicial 
overreach. 
 
Consolidated Construction Consortium v Hitro Energy Solutions Private Limited [2022 | SC | 2J] 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã CCCL was the main contractor for the Chennai Metro Rail project.  
‚û¢‚Äã Hitro Energy (a sole proprietorship) was subcontracted. On CCCL‚Äôs request, CMRL paid ‚Çπ50 lakhs as advance to 
the proprietorship.  
41 
 
‚û¢‚Äã The project was later terminated ‚Üí CCCL refunded the ‚Çπ50L to CMRL and sought reimbursement from the 
proprietorship, which did not pay. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Later, Hitro Energy Solutions Pvt. Ltd. (company) was incorporated ‚Äì its MOA expressly stated that one of its main objects 
was to take over the proprietary concern. 
‚û¢‚Äã CCCL sent a S.8 demand notice and filed S.9 (OC‚ÄìCIRP). 
‚ùñ‚Äã NCLT admitted insolvency ‚Äì BUT ‚Äì NCLAT reversed, holding CCCL was not an OC and the company had not taken over 
the proprietorship. CCCL appealed. 
 
Issues: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Is the debt owed by the proprietary concern or the company (Hitro Pvt Ltd)? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Does the claim qualify as operational debt even though CCCL did not supply, but paid money (receiver, not supplier)? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Is the S.9 application barred by limitation? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Does a pre-existing dispute exist? 
 
Rules: 
‚ùñ‚Äã S.5(21), IBC ‚Üí ‚ÄúClaim in respect of provision of goods or services‚Äù = operational debt 
‚ùñ‚Äã S.8 & S.9, IBC ‚Üí OC may initiate CIRP via demand notice or invoice 
‚ùñ‚Äã S.3(12), IBC ‚Üí Default = non-payment when debt becomes due 
‚ùñ‚Äã S.13, Companies Act 2013 ‚Üí MOA amendment valid only if special resolution is filed and registered with RoC 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã MOA creates binding takeover presumption ‚Üí debt is of the company, not just proprietorship 
‚û¢‚Äã The company‚Äôs MOA explicitly stated takeover of the proprietorship ‚Äì a company cannot escape liability by 
passing an internal board resolution; MOA can only be amended as per S.13, CA 2013 (special resolution + RoC 
filing + registration). 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
No proof of a valid amendment was produced ‚Üí takeover stands ‚Üí debt enforceable against the 
company. 
‚ùñ‚Äã ‚ÄòOperational Debt‚Äô does NOT require the creditor to be a supplier 
‚û¢‚Äã S.5(21) uses ‚Äúin respect of‚Äù ‚Üí requires nexus with goods/services, not supplier status. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The advance of ‚Çπ50L was paid in connection with a service contract (construction subcontract). 
‚û¢‚Äã Even though CCCL was a purchaser/recipient of services (not supplier), the claim has direct nexus to service 
provision ‚Üí qualifies as OD. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Refund of advance payments also qualifies as operational debt 
‚óè‚Äã
Advance paid for a service contract, later repayable, still arises from the service transaction. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Invoice is not mandatory for S.8 notice ‚Üí OC can rely on other proof of debt, not necessarily an invoice. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Limitation runs from date of default, not date of payment 
‚û¢‚Äã Default = when the debt became due and unpaid (S.3(12)). 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
S.9 was filed within 3 years of default ‚Üí within limitation. 
‚ùñ‚Äã No pre-existing dispute 
‚û¢‚Äã Hitro did not dispute the debt on merits; it only said ‚Äúpay CMRL, not us,‚Äù which is not a genuine dispute on 
liability. 
‚û¢‚Äã Under Mobilox, dispute must be real, plausible, and pre-existing in substance, not a feeble legal defence. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Debt is owed by Hitro Energy Solutions Pvt Ltd (MOA takeover valid). 
‚ùñ‚Äã The claim is operational debt under S.5(21) despite CCCL being a service recipient. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Refund of service-linked advance is OD. 
‚ùñ‚Äã S.9 was within limitation. 
‚ùñ‚Äã No pre-existing dispute under Mobilox. 
42 
 
‚ùñ‚Äã CIRP admission restored.
cases:
CIRP Process 
Gujarat Urja Vikas Nigam Limited v Amit Gupta
Arcelormittal India Private Limited v Satish Kumar Gupta
Consolidated Construction Consortium v Hitro Energy Solutions Private Limited
